title: University of Southampton Delhi: India Launch Rationale and Regulatory Experience
text: With a strong track record of producing changemakers & leaders who make a global impact, the University of Southampton's launch is set to change the dynamics of higher education. Read article at India Today With a strong track record of producing changemakers and leaders who make a global impact, the University of Southampton’s launch in India is set to change the dynamics of higher educationA public research university, the University of Southampton, UK, has a very vibrant, welcoming and diverse student community, known for its commitment to academic excellence, quality education, innovation, student success and readiness for the real world. A founding member of the Russell Group, the launch of the first-ever campus of the University of Southampton in India in July last month is a milestone in higher international education. The Delhi-NCR campus will offer Indian students a unique opportunity to earn an international degree without leaving home and learn from the best of the faculty. We sat down with Professor Mark Smith, vice-chancellor, University of Southampton, to learn why India was chosen, what the new campus offers and what students can expect, and how this initiative will shape the future of education and UK-India relations. So, why India? What motivated the University of Southampton to establish a campus here? Well, actually, the better question might be, “Why not India?” The country is experiencing remarkable economic growth and has set out a bold vision to become a global leader by 2047. To achieve that, India needs a highly skilled workforce. It has a vast, talented, and ambitious young population seeking quality education. Crucially, the Indian government’s higher education reforms in 2023 introduced a new policy allowing foreign universities to establish branch campuses. It provided a clear regulatory framework for international universities to set up here, which hadn’t existed before. The combination of strong demand, abundant talent, and a supportive policy environment made India the perfect choice. We are proud to be the first UK university launching a campus in India under this new UGC (University Grants Commission) framework. Were there any challenges navigating the Indian regulatory landscape? On the contrary, I’ve been impressed. The Indian regulators–the University Grants Commission and the Ministry of Education–have been very cooperative. They have clear rules but they’ve been willing to engage, explain those rules, and collaborate. That kind of constructive approach from regulators is rare globally and has made the process smoother and more transparent than I anticipated. It’s encouraging for other international institutions looking to enter India.
summary: The University of Southampton established its Delhi-NCR campus due to India's economic growth, demand for skilled workforce, and supportive 2023 higher education reforms. Vice-Chancellor Mark Smith praised Indian regulators for their cooperative and transparent approach.

title: UoSD Programmes, Admissions, and Enhancing UK-India Academic Collaboration
text: What programmes will the new campus offer initially? We’re starting with six programmes–four undergraduate degrees and two postgraduate degrees–focused on areas with strong demand such as business, economics, accounting, and computer science. Over the next seven to ten years, we plan to expand to about 30 programmes, which is roughly half of what we offer in the UK. Importantly, the curriculum here will be identical to the one back home in Southampton, UK. That’s critical because students graduating here will receive a University of Southampton degree, with the same quality assurance as the UK campus. While we’ll adapt certain elements to reflect local context and skills, the core academic standards and quality assurance processes remain the same. How many students do you expect in the inaugural year, and what is the admission process like? We aim to welcome between 150 and 170 students this September. As we speak, applications and deposits are steadily increasing, so we’re on track. The admission standards are stringent and aligned with our UK standards. For undergraduate courses, applicants typically need two A’s and a B at A-level or equivalent qualifications. For postgraduate programmes, a minimum 2:1 degree classification is required, alongside proficiency in English language. We want to ensure students admitted here are fully prepared to thrive on the same rigorous curriculum as in the UK. How do you see India campus strengthening educational ties between India and the UK? The campus will deepen the existing academic and research collaboration between the two countries. The University of Southampton has a rich history of joint research with Indian institutions; in fact, Indian scholars are among our most frequent international co-authors. By establishing a physical presence in India, we’re embedding these relationships more firmly and paving the way for more UK universities to follow. Indian students gain access to world-class UK education at home, while UK academia benefits from closer collaboration with India’s vibrant research community.
summary: The Delhi campus launches with six undergraduate and postgraduate programmes, aiming for 30, with UK-identical curricula and stringent admissions. It significantly strengthens UK-India academic and research collaboration, offering world-class education and fostering partnerships.

title: UoSD Learning Model, AI Adaptation, Alumni Support, and Prospective Student Guidance
text: How will the learning experience compare with the UK campus or other Indian universities? Students will receive a UK degree taught to identical standards, but the experience will reflect the local context. We’re building strong partnerships with Indian businesses to offer internships and placements, equipping students with practical, real-world skills. Our approach is underpinned by what we call the “triple helix”: a synergy of education, research, and innovation. This philosophy has distinguished Southampton in the UK, and we’re bringing it here. With smaller initial student numbers, we can provide more personalized support and foster close industry engagement. How is the university adapting to the rise of AI and technology in education? AI is evolving rapidly, so we must remain agile. We want students to see AI as a powerful tool, not a shortcut. Developing critical thinking and the ability to ask the right questions is essential. Our curriculum will continuously adapt to incorporate these technologies responsibly, ensuring graduates are well-prepared for future challenges. How strong is the alumni network, especially in India, and how will it benefit students? Our alumni network is a vital source of mentorship, networking, and career guidance. We have about 2,000 alumni in India, and I’m actively engaging them to support our new students. Connecting students with alumni will enrich their learning experience and open doors to opportunities worldwide. Approaching our 75th anniversary, Southampton has a proud legacy of alumni who have made significant global contributions. What advice would you give prospective students applying to the University of Southampton India campus? Aim for academic excellence; our entry requirements are high to maintain quality. Also, be ready for a different learning style that emphasises self-directed study and research. Take initiative and engage actively with your education from the start.
summary: The Delhi campus offers a UK-standard degree with local industry partnerships and emphasizes the "triple helix" of education, research, and innovation. It integrates AI responsibly into curriculum, leverages a strong alumni network in India, and advises students on academic excellence and self-directed learning.
